title: Cynanchica suffruticosa
taxonomy: Cynanchica suffruticosa is classified in the family Apocynaceae, a group of flowering plants commonly known as dogbane or oleander. This classification places it within the order Gentianales, a group of plants characterized by their distinctive flowers and seed pods. At the level of genus, Cynanchica suffruticosa is a member of the genus Cynanchum, which comprises a diverse array of species found in various parts of the world. The family Apocynaceae, to which Cynanchica suffruticosa belongs, is a large and widespread group of plants, with over 4,000 species worldwide. Further classification of Cynanchica suffruticosa at the species level is within the subgenus Cynanchum.
uses: Cynanchica suffruticosa is known to have various medicinal properties. Traditionally, it has been used to treat fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues. The plant's roots and leaves are often used in herbal remedies due to their perceived anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. However, more research is needed to fully understand its medicinal potential. In some cultures, it is also used to treat snakebites and other venomous injuries.
benefits: Cynanchica suffruticosa, also known as the "Yellow Milkweed," has been studied for its potential health benefits. The plant contains compounds that may have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which could help protect against cell damage and reduce inflammation. Some research suggests that extracts from Cynanchica suffruticosa may have antimicrobial effects, which could aid in wound healing and prevention of infections. However, more extensive studies are needed to confirm the efficacy of these potential health benefits. Further research is necessary to fully understand the effects of Cynanchica suffruticosa on human health.

The exact composition of its active constituents is not well-documented, but it is known to contain compounds such as alkaloids and glycosides. Research suggests that Cynanchica suffruticosa may contain bioactive compounds like triterpenoid saponins and flavonoids, which exhibit anti-inflammatory and antioxidant activities. The identification of Cynanchica suffruticosa's active constituents is an area of ongoing research.
